title: Rally Admin Authorities — Vector App Applicability
summary: Rally admin authority model — subscription, workspace, project roles — assessed for Vector app governance design.
content:
Overview

Rally (formerly CA Agile Central, now part of Broadcom's ValueOps suite) is an enterprise agile project management platform used primarily for software delivery at scale. The administration section of Rally's documentation covers everything required to configure and govern a Rally deployment: user management, workspace and project hierarchy, custom fields, work rules, integrations, API security, and data governance. Rally is offered as a SaaS product hosted on Google Cloud Platform (us-east1 and us-central1), with Cloudflare as CDN and network security layer. Subscription tiers include a free Starter Edition (up to 50 users), Team, Enterprise, and Unlimited.

Authority & Permission Model

Three-Tier Administrator Hierarchy

Rally's authority model is organised into three distinct administrator tiers. Each tier is a superset of the one below it — subscription administrators can do everything workspace administrators can, and workspace administrators can do everything project administrators can.

TierScopeAppointed ByDescription
Subscription AdministratorEntire subscription (all workspaces)Broadcom / selfFull control over the subscription. Can promote users to any role, create workspaces, set password policies, manage API keys, and configure HMAC secrets for webhooks.
Workspace AdministratorOne or more assigned workspacesSubscription adminManages custom fields, tags, schedule states, portfolio item types, work rules, and projects within their workspace. Can manage users if delegated permission by the subscription admin.
Project AdministratorOne or more assigned projectsSubscription or workspace adminLimited to team membership, project settings, child project creation, and work rules scoped to their projects. Cannot create new users unless explicitly authorised.

Project-Level Permission Levels

Within a workspace, each user's access to individual projects is set using one of four project permission levels:

PermissionCan Do
No AccessNo visibility to the project (default for all new users unless a workspace default is set).
ViewerRead-only access to the project and all work items within it.
EditorCreate, edit, and delete all work items inside the project. Minimum level required to be a Team Member.
Project AdminAll Editor capabilities plus: project settings, create child projects, manage work item permissions, edit permissions in administrated projects, optionally create new users.

Workspace-Level Permission Levels

PermissionCan Do
No AccessNo visibility to the workspace or any of its projects.
UserAccess to the workspace. Project-level permissions are set separately per project.
Workspace AdminFull administrative control over the workspace.

Key Permission Constraints

  • Permissions are set at workspace and project scope only — there is no artifact-type-level permission granularity. Editors have uniform access to all artifact types within their project.
  • All usernames must be unique across all Rally subscriptions and use email format.
  • Default project permissions for new users can be configured at the workspace level (No Access, Viewer, or Editor). This default applies only to new users — changing it does not retroactively affect existing users.
  • Only subscription administrators can customise user fields, set password policies, delete users, and manage the HMAC secret.

Feature Catalogue

Subscription Configuration

  • Workspace Management: Subscription admins can create multiple workspaces. Each workspace gets a sample project automatically. Workspaces are isolated — rollups and reports do not cross workspace boundaries. Broadcom recommends limiting workspace proliferation for this reason.
  • User Field Customisation: Only subscription admins can add custom fields to user profiles (e.g. Office Location dropdowns). Workspace and project admins cannot create user fields even if they have user-creation permissions.
  • Custom Pages: Admins can create and share custom pages across projects.
  • Webhook Management: Rules that send data to an external server when triggered. Configurable at subscription level.
  • Password Policies: Subscription-admin-only. Can set complexity requirements and expiry rules.
  • HMAC Signature Secret: Subscription-admin-only. Adds SHA256 HMAC digest signing to outbound webhook payloads for consumer verification.
  • mTLS Policy: Subscription-admin-only. Mutual TLS configuration for secure API communication.
  • Disclaimer Message: Subscription admins can add a login disclaimer message.
  • API Key Toggle: Subscription admins can enable or disable API key functionality for the entire subscription.

Workspace Administration

  • Custom Fields: Create, edit, and manage fields for specific work item types. Applied workspace-wide.
  • Tags: Workspace admins can delete, archive, and merge tags for organisational clarity.
  • Schedule States: Customise the built-in state names used for tracking progress.
  • Portfolio Item Types: Configurable labels and hierarchy names for portfolio management.
  • Objectives: Configurable at workspace level for OKR-style tracking.
  • Work Rules: Enforce required, read-only, or must-match field-level rules on work items and portfolio items. Rules can produce errors (blocking) or warnings (non-blocking). Workspace admins can grant project admins the ability to manage work rules via a "Project Admins Can Manage Work Rules" setting.
  • Revision History: View history of workspace-level configuration changes.
  • Time Tracker: Optional feature enablement per workspace.
  • Workspace Details: Name, description, state (Open/Closed), time zone, workdays, date format, work effort units, item ID prefix (alphanumeric).
  • Default User Permissions: Set the default project access level for new users across the workspace.
  • Recycle Bin Purge: Configure automatic purge of deleted work items.
  • Hidden Fields: Workspace and subscription admins can view hidden fields on work items.

Project Administration

  • Project Hierarchy: Projects can nest up to 10 levels deep, though Broadcom recommends a maximum of 3 levels to avoid reporting complexity and performance degradation.
  • Team Membership: Project admins can add existing users to a project as team members. Team members appear in owner dropdowns and the Team Status page; non-members can still be assigned work.
  • Child Project Creation: Project admins can create child projects to build team-of-teams structures.
  • Project Settings: Name, owner, description, state (Open/Closed). Closed projects are preserved but hidden from dropdowns and inaccessible.
  • Workspace Dependency: A project can only be associated with an open workspace.
  • View Scope: Project admins only see the project hierarchies they have access to — they cannot navigate to project detail pages outside their permission scope.

Program Administration

  • Programs: A program is a collection of two or more projects sharing a common business goal and release cadence. Programs have their own backlog.
  • Two Approaches: (1) Basic — project scoping and rollups for multi-team progress views; (2) Advanced — portfolio items with completion percentages, visual indicators, and timeline views.
  • Target Audience: Portfolio Managers, Program Managers, and PMOs.

User Management

  • User Invitation: Subscription admins can invite up to three new users at a time.
  • Password Reset: Subscription-admin-only capability.
  • License Monitoring: Admins can view active license counts and user last-login dates.
  • User Disabling: Both subscription and workspace admins can disable users.
  • User Deletion: Subscription-admin-only.
  • Planner Designation: Workspace and subscription admins can designate users as planners.

IT Administration & Security

  • Infrastructure: Hosted on Google Cloud Platform (us-east1, us-central1). Cloudflare CDN and network security. Broadcom recommends against IP allowlisting due to dynamic IP changes during security incidents.
  • API Keys: Users create their own API keys (Full Access or ALM WSAPI Read-only). Keys inherit the generating user's permissions — no privilege escalation. Keys do not expire but are invalidated when the user is disabled or deleted. Subscription admins can view, delete, and reset all keys in the subscription. API key functionality can be disabled entirely at the subscription level.
  • HMAC Webhook Security: Subscription admins add a secret to the subscription. Outbound webhook payloads are signed with SHA256 HMAC. Consumers validate the Rally-Signature header.
  • Authentication Forms: External login forms POST to https://rally1.rallydev.com/slm/j_spring_security_check.
  • Status Monitoring: System status available at https://rally.status.broadcom.com/.

Sandbox & Data Governance

  • Sandbox: Available to Unlimited and Enterprise Edition subscribers only (not Community or trial). One refresh per quarter, up to four per year. 10 business days to process. No SLA. European datacenter users (eu1) do not have sandbox access.
  • Data Moves: (1) Subscription Merge — moves all data from one subscription into another; (2) Workspace Move — moves a workspace between subscriptions. Considered complex operations; Broadcom recommends engaging a Rally representative first.
  • Integrations Page: Temporarily unavailable as of the crawl date. Subscription admins were previously able to monitor which integrations are active and which users utilise them.

Governance Patterns

Pattern 1: Delegated Authority Chain

Rally enforces a strict top-down delegation model. Subscription admins grant authority to workspace admins, who in turn can grant limited authority to project admins. No admin can grant authority they do not themselves hold. This prevents privilege escalation at every tier boundary.

Pattern 2: Scope Isolation

Workspaces are hard data and reporting boundaries. Rollups, dashboards, and reports never cross workspace boundaries. This forces intentional organisational design — admins must decide upfront whether teams belong in the same workspace (shareable data) or separate workspaces (isolated data).

Pattern 3: Default-Deny Project Access

New users default to "No Access" on all projects unless a workspace-level default is set. Access must be explicitly granted per project. This means users only see what they have been deliberately given access to.

Pattern 4: Uniform Permission Scope

Permissions operate at workspace and project scope — not at artifact type, field, or record level. An editor in a project edits everything in that project. This keeps the permission model simple and comprehensible but limits fine-grained control.

Pattern 5: Work Rules as Policy Enforcement

Rather than encoding policy into permissions, Rally uses Work Rules to enforce field-level governance. Rules can be global (workspace/subscription admin) or local (project admin, if enabled). Rules either block (error) or warn. Admins are responsible for monitoring rule conflicts — Rally does not detect them.

Pattern 6: API Key Governance

API keys inherit user permissions — no service account elevation. Subscription admins can audit and revoke any key in the subscription. The entire API key system can be disabled at the subscription level. This ensures API access is always tied to a human identity with human-scoped permissions.

Pattern 7: Tiered Sandbox Access

Sandbox environments are a subscription-tier feature, not a self-serve option. They are quota-controlled (4 refreshes/year) and have explicit SLA carve-outs. This prevents sandbox sprawl while still enabling safe integration testing.

Vector Applicability Assessment

1. Three-Tier Admin Hierarchy

Rally: Subscription → Workspace → Project admins, each delegated by the tier above.

Vector today: Vector has gadmin (global/technical admin), padmin (portfolio owner/product admin), and user roles. This maps loosely to Rally's subscription/workspace/project split, but the delegation chain is not formally modelled — gadmin grants padmin, padmin operates portfolios.

Apply: Formalise a delegation record. When a gadmin promotes a padmin, record which gadmin granted it and when. When a padmin adds a user to a portfolio, that grant should be auditable. This mirrors Rally's cascading authority model and creates a clean audit trail for the GOV feature area.

2. Default-Deny Project Access with Workspace Defaults

Rally: New users default to No Access on all projects. Workspace admins can set a bulk default (No Access / Viewer / Editor) for new users, with per-project override.

Vector today: Users presumably need explicit portfolio membership to access anything. The question is whether there is a workspace-level default pattern or whether every access grant is done individually.

Apply: Implement a portfolio-level default permission for new tenant users (None / Viewer / Editor). Changing the default should not retroactively affect existing users. This reduces per-user setup friction for large tenant onboarding while maintaining explicit access control.

3. Uniform Scope Permissions with Named Levels

Rally: Four named levels — No Access, Viewer, Editor, Project Admin — with clear, documented capability boundaries per level. No artifact-type granularity, which keeps the model comprehensible.

Vector today: The role model distinguishes padmin vs user but likely does not have a Viewer role — users are either in or out.

Apply: Add a Viewer role (read-only, no create/edit/delete) at portfolio and item scope. Rally's experience shows this is a common requirement: stakeholders who need visibility but must not modify data. Document the capability boundary for each level in the Vector GOV docs.

4. Work Rules as Declarative Policy

Rally: Work Rules enforce field-level constraints independent of permission level. Rules are separate from roles — an editor still cannot violate a work rule. Rules can block or warn. Admins can delegate rule-creation to lower tiers.

Vector today: Field-level validation exists in forms, but declarative workspace-wide policy enforcement (must-have parent, required-before-close, etc.) is not a distinct system.

Apply: Design a Work Rules or Policy Rules system in the RUL feature area. Rules should be: scoped to portfolio or global, toggleable error vs warning mode, created by padmin (portfolio scope) or gadmin (global), and auditable. This is Vector's highest-leverage governance gap relative to Rally.

5. API Key Governance

Rally: Keys inherit user permissions. Subscription admins can audit all keys. Keys are auto-deleted when the user is deleted. The whole system can be disabled by subscription admin.

Vector today: The SEC and API feature areas would cover this. The key patterns to adopt: (a) API keys must inherit the generating user's role and scope — no elevation; (b) gadmin can list and revoke any key in the system; (c) disabling a user must invalidate all their keys.

Apply: If Vector ever exposes an external API (WSAPI equivalent), enforce these three invariants from day one. They are well-proven and prevent common API key abuse patterns.

6. Scope Isolation at the Container Level

Rally: Workspaces are hard reporting and data boundaries. Broadcom explicitly recommends against creating too many workspaces because cross-workspace aggregation is impossible.

Vector today: Portfolios are the primary containers. Cross-portfolio reporting may or may not be intended.

Apply: Make the cross-portfolio reporting boundary an explicit design decision now. Define whether the padmin role spans portfolios or is always scoped to one. Document this in the GOV feature area so the schema and API are designed consistently from the start.

7. Sandbox / Staging Environment Governance

Rally: Sandbox is a subscription-tier feature, quota-controlled, with no SLA. Only Unlimited/Enterprise subscribers get it.

Vector today: As an internal platform, Vector itself is the "subscription." A sandbox pattern applies to tenants who want a non-production space to test configurations, integrations, or workflows.

Apply: When multi-tenancy matures, consider a tenant-level sandbox toggle. Gadmin grants or revokes sandbox access per tenant. Sandbox environments share no data with production. This is a deferred concern but worth noting as a GOV feature area roadmap item.

8. Team Member vs. Access Member Distinction

Rally: "Team Member" is a flag on top of Editor permission. It controls who appears in owner dropdowns and team status views — not what they can do. Non-team-members can still be assigned work.

Vector today: Work item assignment likely surfaces all users with access. As portfolios grow, dropdown noise becomes a UX problem.

Apply: Consider a "Portfolio Member" flag separate from access level. Members appear in assignment dropdowns; non-members (e.g. viewers, stakeholders) do not. This is a UX feature rooted in a governance pattern.

Gaps

  • Integrations page unavailable: The Rally Integrations admin page was explicitly marked "temporarily unavailable" during the crawl. OAuth, third-party connector details, and the integration monitoring dashboard could not be verified.
  • Full 28-row comparison table: The seed page stated 28 administrator comparison rows; only 25 were captured. The missing 3 rows may cover subscription merge, user disabling, and planner designation — all of which were described in prose elsewhere but not confirmed as table rows.
  • SSO/SAML configuration: The IT Administration page did not expose SAML or SSO configuration details. These likely exist in a sub-page of the authentication section that was not successfully fetched.
  • Subscription-tier feature differences: Exact feature differences between Team, Enterprise, and Unlimited tiers were not available in primary Broadcom documentation. Third-party pricing sources (G2, ITQlick) provided tier names but not capability matrices.
  • License types (Full User vs Stakeholder): Rally's license type differentiation (if any beyond seat count) was not confirmed from primary sources. Knowledge base references to "Full User" and "Stakeholder" license classes could not be verified in the crawled docs.
  • mTLS configuration: The mTLS policy setting is listed in the admin capabilities table but no detail page was found during the crawl.
